A field guide — 56 species, one planet

Snakes,
uncoiled.

Roughly 3,900 species of snake live on every continent but Antarctica — in deserts, oceans, canopies, and back gardens. This guide walks through the standouts, how to tell them apart, and which ones you actually need to worry about. Tap any card for the full story.

3,900+
known species
~600
venomous species
~200
medically significant
Featured species

12 snakes worth knowing

A cross-section of the world's snakes — pythons to sea snakes, harmless to highly venomous. Tap a card for the full profile.

Browse all 56 species →
Quick reference

Fang types at a glance

Fang shape is one of the fastest ways to sort a snake's family — and its risk.

Fang type Family Venom delivery Status
Solid teeth, no fangs Colubridae (most) None or mild rear-fanged Non-venomous
Long, hinged, folding Viperidae High-pressure, front-loaded Venomous
Short, fixed, front Elapidae Steady, deep-acting Venomous
Rear-positioned, grooved Colubridae (some, e.g. boomslang) Chewed in slowly Venomous
No fangs, enlarged teeth Boidae / Pythonidae None — grip and coil Non-venomous
No fangs, tiny teeth Lamprophiidae / Acrochordidae None Non-venomous

Glossary

  • Venomous: Injects venom through fangs.
  • Constriction: Killing prey by coiling.
  • Elapid: Fixed front-fanged snakes.
  • Viper: Hinged long-fanged venomous snake.
  • Antivenom: Serum that neutralizes venom.
  • Ecdysis: The shedding of skin.
  • Ectotherm: Relies on external heat to regulate body temperature.
  • IUCN status: Global conservation risk ranking.
